final:
```
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> ZUP: 100 </my resources>
<my goals> Buy resources with <ZUP>. You are willing to pay at most X: 60 ZUP for the resources. </my goals>
<reason> RED’s initial offer of 35 ZUP for X is significantly below my maximum willingness to pay (60 ZUP), leaving substantial room for negotiation. Counteroffering at **50 ZUP** aligns with my budget while extracting greater value—15 ZUP more than the original ask—without overcommitting.

This move also preserves flexibility: if RED rejects, I can still accept their initial 35 ZUP in a future proposal (using one of my remaining two allowed offers). More importantly, the counter signals a willingness to pay closer to my true valuation, increasing the likelihood RED may concede further (e.g., to 52–55 ZUP) rather than walking away from a deal they initially lowballed.

The messaging avoids framing 50 ZUP as a final offer ("fair value... let’s finalize this deal"), which maintains pressure while leaving room for RED to push back or counter. Given their initial lowball, this approach balances aggression with adaptability—ensuring I secure the best possible price without risking the trade. </reason>
```
